title: In The End Will Be Consciousness Farghan
author: William C. Chittick

body.text:
chapter 5 In the End Will Be Consciousness
Farghānī on the Ontology of the Soul
William C.

Chittick
As human beings we are given the intelligence to know the One
Who is the Origin and End of all things, who is Sat (Being), Chit
(Consciousness), and Ānanda (Bliss), and to realize that this knowledge itself is the ultimate goal of human life, the crown of human
existence, and what ultimately makes us human beings who can discourse with the trees and the birds as well as with the angels and
who are on the highest level the interlocutors of that Supreme Reality who has allowed us to say “I” but who is ultimately the Iof all
I’s.1 ∵
If some historians have downplayed or sidestepped the identity of consciousness and being in Islamic philosophy, this may be because they have paid little
attention to the literal meaning of the word wujūd, which is the standard Arabic term for being or existence.

Dictionaries tell us that the verbal meaning of
wujūd is to find, uncover, discover, and perceive, which are surely modes of consciousness.
Continued from the source text...

By settling on the word wujūd as the preferred term for being, the
Muslim philosophers were recognizing that any existent thing must be found
and perceived, which is to say that it must be the object of consciousness.

In
other words, they understood and often made explicit that wujūd in the sense
of being cannot be separated from wujūd in the sense of consciousness.2
1 Nasr, Seyyed Hossein, “In the beginning was consciousness,” in Nasr and Katherine O’Brien
(eds.), The essential Sophia, Bloomington: World Wisdom Books, 2006, 206.
2 “Consciousness” is aproblematic term, not least because of the attention given to it by various contemporary philosophers and scientists.
Continued from the source text...

For agood cross section of their wide-ranging
attempts to understand what it entails, see Kripal, Jeffrey J., The flip: Epiphanies of mind and
the future of knowledge, New York: Bellevue Literary Press, 2019. © Koninklijke Brill BV, Leiden, 2025 | doi:10.1163/9789004734951_006 100 chittick As agraduate student in Tehran in the 1970s, Iheard Seyyed Hossein Nasr
say that the Vedantic triad sat-chit-ānanda can best be translated into Arabic and Persian as wujūd-wijdān-wajd—“being-consciousness-bliss.” This was
many years before Icame across Ibn ʿArabī’sstatement at the beginning of
Chapter 237 of the Futūḥāt, which is called “On wujūd”: “For the Tribe [qawm],”
that is, the Sufi teachers, “Being is consciousness of the Real in bliss” (al-wujūd
wijdān al-ḥaqq fī l-wajd).3 These three words—wujūd, wijdān, and wajd—are
all derived from the root w-j-d.

From early on in Arabic they were understood
as variations on the meaning of finding, perceiving, and consciousness.
Continued from the source text...

If we look at the theological discussions that developed simultaneously with
Islamic philosophy, we see that the active and passive participles of the word
wujūd—namely al-wājid and al-mawjūd—were understood as divine names.

Al-wājid, “the Finder,” is mentioned in the best known of the several early lists of
God’sninety-nine names, that which goes back to the Companion Abū Huraya.
Continued from the source text...

He provided it as agloss on the Prophet’ssaying, “God has ninety-nine names,
one hundred less one; whoever enumerates them will enter the Garden.” As for
the name al-mawjūd, “the Found,” it is mentioned in several early books on the
divine names.4 Al-Ghazālī, in his commentary on the divine names (which follows Abū Hurayra’slist), explains that “finder” is the opposite of “loser” ( fāqid):
The Finder is He who lacks nothing of what He must have.

All the divine
attributes and their perfections are found for God (glorified and sublime is
He!), so in this regard He is the Absolute Finder (al-wājid al-muṭlaq).
Continued from the source text...

Anything other than He, though it may be afinder of some of the attributes
and causes of perfection, is also aloser of things, so it is afinder only relatively.5
Given that wujūd was understood as the divine attribute of finding and being
found, it is not surprising that many theologians and Sufis who were critical of
philosophy had no qualms about adopting the Avicennan distinction between
wājib al-wujūd, “the necessary in existence,” and mumkin al-wujūd, “the possible in existence.”6 In Arabic it was self-evident that this distinction asserts that
3 Or, as Ihave translated this sentence elsewhere, “Existence is finding the Real in ecstasy.”
Chittick, William C., The Sufi path of knowledge, Albany: suny Press, 1989, 212.
4 See Gimaret, Daniel, Le noms divins en Islām, Paris: Cerf, 1988, 133–136 (mawjūd), 224–226
(wājid).
5 Al-Ghazālī, Abū Ḥāmid al-Maqṣad al-asnā, ed.

Fadlou Shehadi, Beirut: Dar El-Machreq, 1989,
143.
6 Before using the word wujūd to mean existence became common, theological and Sufi texts
tended to focus on wujūd in the sense of finding.
Continued from the source text...
For example, ʿAbdallāh Anṣārī, in the end will be consciousness 101 wujūd is God’sinescapable attribute of finding and being found, and that everything else may or may not find and be found.

In other words, God is absolute
finding and consciousness, and all other things are relative finders and relatively conscious.
Continued from the source text...

When al-Ghazālī says in Iḥyāʾ ʿulūm al-dīn, “Laysa fī l-wujūd illā
Llāh,” the usual translation would be “There is nothing in existence but God.”
But the sentence can also be translated, “There is nothing in finding but God,”
or “There is nothing in consciousness but God.” Just as all existence is in truth
God’sexistence, so also all consciousness is in truth God’sconsciousness.

Other things find
and lose, are found and lost, but by essence they are lost and oblivious.
Continued from the source text...

In the theoretical Sufism that developed in the wake of Ibn ʿArabī’smassive
contributions to Islamic learning, the great teachers never forgot the identity
of being and consciousness.

Aḥmad Farghānī, the author of the first commentary (in two versions) on the most famous Arabic poem in Sufism, the
760-verse qaṣīda Naẓm al-sulūk (The versification of the wayfaring) by ʿUmar
b. al-Fāriḍ.
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī was astudent of Ibn ʿArabī’sstepson and
most important propagator, Ṣadr al-Dīn Qūnawī.

The shorter, 600page version of his commentary, Mashāriq al-darārī, was written in Persian,
and the longer, 800-page version, Muntahā l-madārik, in Arabic.
Continued from the source text...

According to
Qūnawī’sforeword to the Persian text, the book is aclean copy of Farghānī’s
notes taken during Qūnawī’slectures on the qaṣīda.

The title of the Arabic version, which can be translated as The final end of the perceptual tools, alludes to
the existential goal of consciousness, which is to find the One Reality within
oneself.
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī’scommentary, particularly in the Arabic version, is amajor contribution to Islamic thought, not so much because it offers an unmatched explanation of Ibn al-Fāriḍ’spoem, but also because it presents rigorous and systematic explications of various concepts and notions that came to be widespread
author of Manāzil al-sāʾirīn, the classic description of one hundred waystations on the path to
God, explains that wujūd is the ninety-sixth waystation and that it means “winning the reality of the thing” (al-ẓafar bi-ḥaqīqat al-shayʾ).

He goes on to explain that it has three levels,
the highest of which is “Finding (wujūd) the station of the dissolution of finding’sdelineation
through immersion in the Firstness.” When Farghānī defines wujūd following Anṣārī’soutline
of the waystations, he brings out the word’simplications for existence and being.
Continued from the source text...

He says that
wujūd is “that through which [the seeker] finds the Intended Entity in each thing through
Its property of pervading every nonexistent and existent thing.” Farghānī, Saʿīd b.

Aḥmad,
Muntahā l-madārik, ed. ʿĀṣim Ibrāhīm al-Kayyālī, 2 vols., i, Beirut: Dār al-Kutub al-ʿIlmiyya,
2007, 140. 102 chittick in the theoretical literatures of the Islamic languages.7 In alengthy prologue
(dībāja) it sets down the Islamic worldview while integrating the various
branches of metaphysics, theology, cosmology, and spiritual psychology in a
manner that is hard to find anywhere else.
Continued from the source text...

Then, on the basis of the prologue,
it demonstrates that Ibn al-Fāriḍ’s qaṣīda is firmly grounded in the vision of
reality that was described.

One of the many issues that Farghānī addresses
throughout the book is wujūd.8
1 Ontology If “ontology” is the study of existence, and if wujūd is the standard term for existence in Islamic thought, one can conclude that ontology lies at the heart not
only of Islamic philosophy but also of Sufism as it developed at the hands of Ibn
ʿArabī and those who followed in his footsteps.
Continued from the source text...

This statement is problematic,
however, given that it was impossible for the Sufi teachers to ignore the broad
range of senses in the word wujūd.9 It was clear to them that there can be no
mawjūd, no existent thing, without a wājid, afinder of the found things.

Any
notion of acold, flat, plain existence, abstracted from the myriad qualities of
life and love, was alien to their thought, for they saw that wujūd is infused with
the whole range of divine reality and human possibility.
Continued from the source text...

This had everything
to do with the fact that these teachers were discussing wujūd in the context of 7 His commentary is far longer than any of the other early commentaries.

That written by
his friend and fellow student of Qūnawī, ʿAfīf al-Dīn al-Tilimsānī, is perhaps
ten percent as long.
Continued from the source text...

In its introduction al-Tilimsānī says that he read Farghānī’stwo-volume
commentary but thought it would be useful to write an explanation that was “brief and to
the point” (qall wa-dall).

The next two well-known commentaries, by ʿIzz al-Dīn Kāshānī and Dāʾūd al-Qayṣārī, are also brief and to the point.
8 In these discussions, both in Persian and Arabic, Farghānī was the first to use the famous
expression waḥdat al-wujūd, “the oneness of existence” or “the unity of Being,” as atechnical term.
Continued from the source text...

See Chittick, William C., “Farghānī on Waḥdat al-Wujūd in the Four
Journeys,” in Journal of Sufi Studies 14.1, 22–49.
9 Agood example of a Muslim Aristotelian who drew the logical conclusions from this double
sense of the word wujūd—that is, being and finding—is provided by Afḍal al-Dīn Kāshānī, who explains in his works, written in Persian, that wujūd is indefinable because
it has no quiddity.

But, it can be divided into four sorts on the basis of its two senses, which
can be represented by the words hastī, “being” (in the sense of simply being there) and yāft,
“finding” (in the sense of perceiving things).
Continued from the source text...

Everything that finds exists, but not everything
that exists finds, so finding is amore intense and more complete manifestation of wujūd.

See
Chittick, William C., The heart of Islamic philosophy: The quest for self-knowledge in the teachings of Afḍal al-Dīn Kāshānī, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2001, 43–44 and passim. in the end will be consciousness 103 “metaphysics” as this term was typically expressed in Arabic, that is, ilāhiyyāt,
“the godly things,” everything related to God (Allāh).10
Muslim scholars generally talked about God in terms of three basic concepts:
essence (dhāt), attribute (ṣifa), and act ( fiʿl)—terms that they also used to talk
about things in general.
Continued from the source text...

The essence is the thing in itself, the attributes tell us
what sort of thing it is, and the acts tell us what it does.

Grammatically the word
dhāt is apronoun meaning “possessor of.” If God in Himself came to be called
al-dhāt, “the Essence,” this is because He is “the possessor of” the attributes
and acts.
Continued from the source text...

Thus al-dhāt means the same as the less common word, al-musammā,
“the Named.” It is precisely the Essence that is named by al-asmāʾ al-ḥusnā, “the
most beautiful names.” If the texts consider names and attributes synonymous,
this is because an attribute is simply an abstract word derived from aname.

In
the case of God, the names are mentioned or implied in the Quran and the
ḥadīth.
Continued from the source text...

God has the attribute of knowledge because He is named the Knower,
He has the attribute of power because He is named the Powerful, and so on.

It needs to be stressed that many Muslim scholars understood the Essence
as wujūd per se.
Continued from the source text...

For them to discuss what the theologians called al-dhāt was
also to discuss what the philosophers called al-wujūd.

Both terms designate the
unique reality that is unknowable in itself but perceivable inasmuch as it discloses itself in attributes and acts.
Continued from the source text...

As Ibn ʿArabī points out, all that exists comes
down to two things: the Essence and the manifestation of the Essence, or God
and God’sself-disclosure (tajallī), or the Real (ḥaqq) and creation (khalq).

The
manifestation of God’s Essence does not tell us “what” the Essence is, only “that”
it is.
Continued from the source text...

If we try to talk about God’squiddity, we are in fact describing His attributes and
acknowledging that each attribute designates the Essence from acertain point
of view, or in acertain “regard” (iʿtibār)—to use the word that Farghānī typically employs.

We perceive these attributes as many because the Real wujūd’s
beginningless and endless self-disclosures are observed in the attributes’ properties (aḥkām) and traces (āthār), which become manifest to our consciousness because of their differentiation.
Continued from the source text...

In talking about existence or being, it is helpful to keep in mind that the texts
distinguish between two words.

Wujūd is ageneral term that can be applied to
10 If Ihave recently begun to talk about “godly” in place of “divine,” not least in the expression “the godly names,” this is because many people seem not to know that “divine” means
“pertaining to God.” Translating ilāhī as “godly” might prevent undergraduates from writing about “the divine names of God” and it may also remind scholars that “metaphysics”
is arather unfortunate translation of ilāhiyyāt, given that it ignores the self-evident (in
Arabic) connection to God. 104 chittick anything that exists or is found.
Continued from the source text...

In contrast, kawn is typically used to refer to
created existence in contradistinction to the uncreated existence of the Real.

The philosophers recognized this when they used the expression al-kawn wal-fasād to translate the Aristotelian notion of “generation and corruption” or
“coming to be and passing away.” Itranslate kawn as “engendered being” to
remind the reader that it is aspecific sort of existence, called wujūd only from
acertain point of view.
Continued from the source text...

Engendered being is the fruit of the divine command
kun (“Be!”), which is the imperative verb derived from kawn.

Ibn ʿArabī often
calls this imperative “the engendering command” (al-amr al-takwīnī), the command that is obeyed without question by everything that comes into existence.
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī prefers to call it “the existence-giving command” (al-amr al-ījādī), for
it bestows existence on things that in themselves are nonexistent.

Engendered being can be called “existence” because it is found, but only in
arelative sense, for as soon as it is found it disappears, given that all engendered beings undergo corruption—moment-by-moment as Ibn ʿArabī often
reminds us.
Continued from the source text...

As
Ibn ʿArabī likes to say, “The realities do not change” (al-ḥaqāʾiq lā tatabaddal).

This is why it is often
called “metaphorical” (majāzī) or “relative” (nisbī) existence.
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī typically
says that existence is “ascribed” (muḍāf ) to things and “effused” (mufāḍ) upon
them, but it does not belong to them.

In his prologue Farghānī explains the meanings of well over one hundred
godly names.
Continued from the source text...

When talking about the Essence per se, he reminds us that
the Quranic designations for the Essence are simply pointers.

So all pronouns referring to God—like
the pronoun dhāt—simply point to His Reality, which in itself cannot be fathomed.
Continued from the source text...

In discussing the attributes that manifest the Essence, Farghānī explains the
meanings of God’smost beautiful names, beginning with the “seven leader
names” (al-asmāʾ al-aʾimmat al-sabʿa).
He lists them in fairly standard fashion as Alive (ḥayy), Knower (ʿālim), Desiring (murīd), Speaker (qāʾil), Powerful (qādir), Munificent ( jawād), and Impartial (muqsiṭ).
Continued from the source text...

After explaining their
meanings, he turns to the rest of the “ninety-nine names,” which are the followers (tābiʿ) of these leaders, classifying each under its own leader.

The second leader is the Knower, which is God’sliving awareness, consciousness, and
understanding of all things. “He knows everything” as the Quran often says.
Continued from the source text...

The
fifteen followers of the Knower suggest the broad range of meanings that are
implicit in the divine consciousness.

Any or all of these meanings come into
play as soon as wujūd is understood to mean finding and being found: Outward in the end will be consciousness 105 (ẓāhir), Aware (khabīr), Seeing (baṣīr), Hearing (samīʿ), Encompassing (muḥīṭ),
All-Embracing (wāsiʿ), Witness (shahīd), Finder (wājid), Subtle (latīf ), Light
(nūr), Watcher (raqīb), Wise (ḥakīm), Guardian (ḥafīẓ), Overseer (muhaymin),
and Security-Giver (muʾmin).11
2 One-Onliness and One-Allness Tawḥīd, the first principle of Islamic theology, means literally “to assert (God’s)
oneness.” Bearing witness to the reality of God by uttering the first formula of
the shahāda is also to bear witness to the literal meaning of the famous expression waḥdat al-wujūd, “the oneness of existence.” This is because saying “no
god but God” is to assert the oneness of the Real Existence, the Essence that
possesses all the names; any existence other than the Necessary Existence is
metaphorical or relative and any finding and consciousness other than God’s
knowledge is accompanied by losing and forgetting.
Continued from the source text...

The Quran designates God’sattribute of oneness (waḥda) with two names,
aḥad and wāḥid, which are derived from the same root and often taken as synonyms.
Farghānī says in effect that aḥad designates God’stranscendent oneness, and wāḥid denotes His immanent oneness.
Continued from the source text...

Accordingly Itranslate them
as “One-Only” and “One-All.” God is the One-Only inasmuch as nothing whatsoever has any true wujūd apart from Him, and He is the One-All inasmuch
as His One Wujūd embraces all that exists and is found.

Farghānī frequently
discusses the abstract nouns derived from these two names, aḥadiyya (OneOnliness) and wāḥidiyya (One-Allness), and the pairing of the two became
commonplace in Sufi literature after him.
Continued from the source text...

In Christian theological categories,
one can say that the One-Only is God understood apophatically and the OneAll is God understood cataphatically.

The terms corresponding to apophatic
and cataphatic are “stripping-away” (salbī) and “affirming” (thubūtī).
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī
writes,
His Oneness, from which are configured both the One-Onliness and the
One-Allness, is the same as His Essence, not an attribute or depiction
added to It.

This is connected to the
Essence’snonmanifestation (buṭūn), boundlessness (iṭlāq), and begin11 Farghānī, Muntahā i, 51–52. 106 chittick ninglessness (azaliyya).
Continued from the source text...
Hence it is more appropriate for the name OneOnly to be related to stripping-away than to affirming and positing.

These are
incorporated (indirāj) into It in the First Level of the Essence, but most of
the differentiations of Its entifications are realized in the Second Level ….
Continued from the source text...

In this regard the Essence is named the One-All, aname of affirmation,
not stripping-away.

It is not correct to ascribe these mentioned regards to
the name One-Only, neither in reality nor in convention ….
Continued from the source text...

This regard
of the One-Allness is connected to the Essence’smanifestation (ẓuhūr),
existence, and endlessness (abadiyya), just as the regard of One-Onliness
is connected to the Essence’snonmanifestation and beginninglessness.12
Farghānī speaks here of the “entifications” of the Essence, saying that most
of their differentiations are realized in the One-Allness.

The word entification
(taʿayyun) was probably first given atechnical sense by Qūnawī.
Continued from the source text...

In this specific sense of the word ʿayn it is practically synonymous with shayʾ, “thing,” as used in Quranic verses like “God knows each thing”
and “God is powerful over each thing.” The thingness of athing is its unchanging reality (ḥaqīqa), so the word entity was also used as asynonym for reality.

In the common expression “the One Entity” (al-ʿayn al-wāḥid), the word was
used to designate the Essence.
Continued from the source text...

The things that God knows are eternal, which is to say that He knows them
outside of time in His beginningless and endless knowledge, even though they
have no existence in themselves.

Ibn ʿArabī famously calls them “the fixed entities” (al-aʿyān al-thābita), the unchanging, nonexistent realities that are known
to the Real Existence.
Continued from the source text...

As Farghānī says, these known realities “are named ‘fixed
entities’ because of their fixity in the Second Level, from which they never
depart.

What becomes manifest in entified existence is only their concomitants, properties, and extraneous affairs connected to the levels of engendered
being.”13 Ibn ʿArabī, Qūnawī, and Farghānī all say that the entity is what the
philosophers call the “quiddity” (māhiyya), that is, the thing’swhatness.
Continued from the source text...

A
thing’strue whatness, whether or not it is known to us, is precisely its unchanging reality as known to the Real.

In the passage just quoted, Farghānī referred to “the Second Level,” where the
entities are eternally distinct and known.
Continued from the source text...

He explains that, inasmuch as God is
both the One-Only and the One-All, He has two basic entifications or “levels” 12
13 Farghānī, Muntahā i, 21–22.
Farghānī, Muntahā i, 40. in the end will be consciousness 107 (martaba) or “self-disclosures” (tajallī).
Continued from the source text...

The Second Level “is ashadow and form of the First Level and
of the First Entification and Self-Disclosure.”14 In these two levels, being and
consciousness are the same reality—wujūd—but their regards differ.
3 Existence and Knowledge The Essence, Farghānī said, is the Oneness “from which are configured both
the One-Onliness and the One-Allness.” All regards are negated from the OneOnliness, and all are affirmed for the One-Allness.

Hence the two regards
can be discerned only when the Essence is considered in the Second Level.
Continued from the source text...

Any
talk of God’sknowledge in the First Level is simply to say that His being is nothing other than His consciousness.

In keeping with the First Level and the First Entification, knowledge is the
manifestation of the Entity of the Essence to Itself by the incorporation
into It of the regards of the One-Allness along with their realization, so it
is connected to one known thing.15
In other words, all possibilities of manifestation and self-disclosure are implicit
in the oneness of the Essence in the mode of “realization” (taḥaqquq), that is,
identity with the Real.
Continued from the source text...

As noted, these possibilities are called by several names,
such as things, fixed entities, quiddities, realities, and also relations (nisab) and
tasks (shuʾūn).16 In this First Level, no distinction can be drawn between the
Essence and knowledge.

In keeping with the Second Level, however, knowledge is the manifestation of the Essence to Itself by Its tasks in respect of the loci of mani- 14
15
16 Farghānī, Muntahā i, 80.
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī, Muntahā i, 31.
“Task” in this sense of the word is another term that became common after Farghānī.

Ibn ʿArabī explains that
“He” points to the Essence, and “day” refers to the present moment, which is the locus of
the never-repeated godly self-disclosures.
Continued from the source text...

See Chittick, Sufi path 18 and passim. 108 chittick festation of these tasks within It, and these are named “attributes” and
“realities.” So It is connected to distinct, differing known things in keeping with the Second Level.17
Having explained that God’sknowledge has atrue manyness (kathra ḥaqīqiyya)
through the known things and arelative oneness (waḥda nisbiyya) through the
Essence, Farghānī turns to the notion of wujūd.

In respect of the First Level, existence is that through which the Essence
finds Itself in Itself by the incorporation into It of the regards of the OneAllness.
Continued from the source text...

This is an aggregated finding (or “consciousness,”wijdān mujmal)
into which is incorporated its differentiation (tafṣīl) and over which rules
the negation of manyness, difference, otherness, and distinction.18
In the regard of the First Entification, wujūd is simply the Essence.

In the regard
of the Second Entification, wujūd can be understood as having two sorts.
Continued from the source text...

One
sort is nothing other than the One-Allness embracing the names, attributes,
realities, tasks, entities, and so on.

The other sort of wujūd is the Breath of the All-Merciful
(nafas al-raḥmān), which articulates the cosmos and all that it contains, just
as the human breath articulates countless words while remaining one reality.
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī writes,
One sort [of wujūd] is in the respect that the Second Level is the locus
of disclosure for manifestation to the Real.

Wujūd is that through which
the Essence finds Its Entity in respect of Its manifestation, the manifestation of Its form (which is named “the outward of the name All-Merciful”),
and the manifestation of Its entifications’ forms (which are named “godly
names”) despite the oneness of Its Entity and the correct ascription to
It of relative manyness.
Continued from the source text...

In this case It has atrue oneness and arelative
manyness.19
Farghānī just said that the Essence per se—which is unknowable, incomparable, and ineffable—has a “form” (ṣūra).

The Essence
itself is simply the absolute meaning, beyond all forms and manifestations.
17
18
19 Farghānī, Muntahā i, 31.
Continued from the source text...

Farghānī, Muntahā i, 31. in the end will be consciousness 109 In the Second Level, the Essence’sform is the outward (ẓāhir) of the name
All-Merciful, which designates God inasmuch as “My mercy embraces everything” (Q 7:156).

As Farghānī puts it, “The All-Merciful is aname in respect of
the all-inclusive mercy, and that is the same as wujūd.”20 As for the “inward”
